questions.text: On Bitcoin's public blockchain, if someone knows your wallet address, what can they see?

options.text: Only transactions made in the last 30 days
Your complete transaction history, current balance, and every address you've interacted with - from the blockchain's inception
Only incoming transactions - outgoing transactions are hidden

questions.explanation: Public blockchains are fully transparent. Any address's complete transaction history - every incoming and outgoing transaction, exact amounts, timestamps, counterparty addresses, and current balance - is visible to anyone with internet access, from the very first transaction associated with that address. This is what "transparent" means. The only thing that is NOT visible (without an external link) is whose address it is.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C - Explanation

Public blockchains are fully transparent. Any address's complete transaction history - every incoming and outgoing transaction, exact amounts, timestamps, counterparty addresses, and current balance - is visible to anyone with internet access, from the very first transaction associated with that address. This is what "transparent" means. The only thing that is NOT visible (without an external link) is whose address it is.

questions.text: "Pseudo-anonymity" in the context of public blockchains is BEST described as:

options.text: Complete anonymity - it is impossible to link any transaction to a real person
Full transparency - every transaction is linked to a verified real-world identity
Transactions are publicly visible, but the identity behind an address is not inherently revealed - though it can be linked through external means
Only validators and miners can see transaction details

questions.explanation: Pseudo-anonymity means the transactions are fully public (anyone can see amounts, addresses, timestamps) but the addresses are pseudonyms - they don't inherently reveal who controls them. The "pseudo" part means it's not true anonymity: the identity can be linked through KYC on exchanges, public address sharing, IP address logging, or transaction graph analysis. Once linked, the entire history is exposed retroactively.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C - Explanation

Pseudo-anonymity means the transactions are fully public (anyone can see amounts, addresses, timestamps) but the addresses are pseudonyms - they don't inherently reveal who controls them. The "pseudo" part means it's not true anonymity: the identity can be linked through KYC on exchanges, public address sharing, IP address logging, or transaction graph analysis. Once linked, the entire history is exposed retroactively.

questions.text: What is a "block explorer" (such as MSTScan for MST Blockchain)?

options.text: A tool used by miners to find valid block hashes faster
A private dashboard for validators to monitor the network
A publicly accessible web interface for searching and reading blockchain data - transactions, wallet balances, blocks, and smart contracts
A wallet application for storing and sending cryptocurrency

questions.explanation: A block explorer is essentially a search engine and viewer for blockchain data. No login, no fee, no permission required. Examples: MSTScan (MST), Etherscan (Ethereum), Blockchain.com (Bitcoin). Anyone can look up any transaction hash, any wallet address, any block number, and any smart contract - viewing complete data going back to the genesis block. This is the tool that makes blockchain's transparency property practically accessible.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C - Explanation

A block explorer is essentially a search engine and viewer for blockchain data. No login, no fee, no permission required. Examples: MSTScan (MST), Etherscan (Ethereum), Blockchain.com (Bitcoin). Anyone can look up any transaction hash, any wallet address, any block number, and any smart contract - viewing complete data going back to the genesis block. This is the tool that makes blockchain's transparency property practically accessible.

questions.text: Rahul publicly posts his Bitcoin wallet address on his Instagram profile to receive donations. What privacy risk does this create?

options.text: No risk - Bitcoin addresses don't reveal personal information
His private key may be exposed if someone analyses the address
His real identity (Instagram profile) is now permanently linked to his Bitcoin address, exposing his complete transaction history to anyone who looks it up
His wallet may be hacked because his address is public

questions.explanation: By posting his Bitcoin address on his Instagram profile, Rahul has created a permanent, public link between his real-world identity (his Instagram account - which has his name, photo, and followers) and his blockchain address. Anyone who views his Instagram now knows his Bitcoin address. From that address, they can see his complete transaction history, total balance, all incoming donors, all outgoing transactions - everything. This link cannot be undone. Even if Rahul deletes the post, the information has already been seen and may be cached. Option B is wrong - public keys/addresses cannot reveal private keys (that would break elliptic curve cryptography).
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C - Explanation

By posting his Bitcoin address on his Instagram profile, Rahul has created a permanent, public link between his real-world identity (his Instagram account - which has his name, photo, and followers) and his blockchain address. Anyone who views his Instagram now knows his Bitcoin address. From that address, they can see his complete transaction history, total balance, all incoming donors, all outgoing transactions - everything. This link cannot be undone. Even if Rahul deletes the post, the information has already been seen and may be cached. Option B is wrong - public keys/addresses cannot reveal private keys (that would break elliptic curve cryptography).

questions.text: "Using a different Bitcoin wallet address for every transaction makes a user completely untraceable - their transactions cannot be linked to each other or to their real identity."
questions.explanation: 1 mark: States FALSE and provides a coherent justification mentioning at least one de-anonymisation technique - input clustering, KYC linkage, IP logging, or transaction graph analysis.
